summary: Joel describes his experiment with enforcing a strict 9-to-5 workday, inspired by Tom DeMarco's book 'Slack' and its advocacy for shorter workdays. He theorizes that limiting work hours forces you to eliminate wasted time. He also reviews 'Slack' itself, praising its core idea but criticizing it as a book that should have been an article, with diminishing returns after the first few chapters. The post ends with a tech interview puzzle and a self-deprecating note about his new schedule slowing down side projects.
key_points: Forcing yourself to leave after 8 hours may train you to stop wasting time during the day
DeMarco's 'Slack' advocates for 7.5-hour workdays, reinforcing ideas from 'Peopleware' about knowledge work
Lister's Law: you can't think faster, so factory-style productivity pressure doesn't work for knowledge workers
'Slack' is criticized as a one-idea book padded into book length — it should have been an article
money_quotes: If you force yourself to leave work after 8 hours, eventually you train yourself not to waste time during the day.
Slack itself is one of those books that should have been an article.
You can't think faster, so trying to force knowledge workers to work "faster" in the same way you try to get Nike shoe makers to make more shoes per hour will never work.
It devolves into a typical lightweight bizniss who-moved-that-cheese kind of book about Managing Change, albeit an intelligent one.
key_insight: Knowledge work productivity comes not from longer hours but from disciplined, focused shorter ones — you can't think faster, only think better.
